title: Does It Matter Where You Go to School If Your Industry Only Cares About Skills?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have heard the advice repeated across forums, social media, and career panels: skills matter more than degrees. Your portfolio is more important than your diploma. Companies are dropping degree requirements. The specific name on your transcript does not determine your future. This advice is appealing, especially if you are not attending a prestigious school. It suggests the playing field is level and your work will speak for itself. The reality is more complicated. Skills matter enormously, but the institutional brand name on your diploma still opens doors that skills alone may not. Here is an honest look at when prestige matters, when it does not, and how to build a career regardless of where you go to school.

Where Prestige Still Matters

In some industries, the name on your diploma functions as a credential that unlocks access. Consulting firms, investment banks, and top law firms recruit heavily from a small number of elite schools. These employers do not necessarily believe that students at these schools are smarter or better prepared. They recruit there because the schools have already done the filtering for them. The admissions process at a highly selective university screens for the traits these employers value. Recruiting at these schools is efficient. The talent is concentrated. The employers know what they are getting.

This does not mean you cannot break into these industries from a less prestigious school. It means the path is different. At an elite school, recruiters come to you. They attend career fairs, host information sessions, and conduct on-campus interviews. At a less prestigious school, you go to the recruiters. You network your way in. You apply through online portals rather than through campus pipelines. The destination is the same. The route is longer and requires more initiative.

Graduate school admissions also show a prestige bias. PhD programs, medical schools, and law schools consider the reputation of your undergraduate institution as one factor among many. A strong GPA from an elite school is weighted more heavily than a strong GPA from a lesser-known school. The assumption is that grading standards are more rigorous and the peer group is more competitive. This assumption is not always fair, but it exists. If you are targeting a competitive graduate program, the prestige of your undergraduate institution will be part of the calculus, whether you like it or not.

Where Skills Dominate

In technology, the prestige bias has weakened significantly. Major tech companies now hire from a wide range of schools, including bootcamps and self-taught programmers. The technical interview is the great equalizer. If you can solve the problems, you get the job. The name on your diploma does not help you pass a coding challenge. Your skills do. This is one reason the technology industry has been a leader in reducing degree requirements and expanding hiring pipelines.

In creative fields, portfolios rule. A graphic designer, a writer, a filmmaker, or a musician is evaluated on the quality of their work, not the name of their school. A stunning portfolio from a state school graduate will beat a mediocre portfolio from an Ivy League graduate every time. The work is the credential. The degree is secondary.

In skilled trades and technical fields, certification and demonstrated competence matter more than institutional prestige. An electrician with a license and years of experience does not need a prestigious degree. The license is the credential. The experience is the proof. Many of these fields offer excellent compensation without requiring a traditional four-year degree at all.

What Prestige Actually Buys You

The value of a prestigious school is not primarily the education. The quality of teaching at elite schools is not necessarily better than at other institutions. The value is in three things: the network, the signaling effect, and the recruiting pipelines.

The network is the collection of classmates, alumni, and faculty connections that can open doors throughout your career. A student at a school with a powerful alumni network has access to introductions, informational interviews, and job opportunities that a student without that network must work harder to find.

The signaling effect is the shortcut that the name provides. An employer reviewing hundreds of resumes sees a degree from a school they recognize and respect. They assume a certain level of competence. That assumption may not be accurate, but it is real. The signal gets your resume read more carefully. It gets you the benefit of the doubt.

The recruiting pipelines are the structured pathways from campus to career. On-campus interviews, career fairs attended by top employers, alumni referral programs. These pipelines make the job search more efficient. They reduce the friction between graduation and employment.

None of these advantages are insurmountable. A student at any school can build a network, develop a strong signal through work and credentials, and find their own path to employers. It just takes more work. The prestige gap is not a gap in capability. It is a gap in the ease of access.

How to Compete From Any School

Build a portfolio that demonstrates your skills independently of your degree. A GitHub profile with substantial projects. A writing portfolio with published clips. A design portfolio with client work. The portfolio is your credential when the degree is not doing the work for you.

Network aggressively and intentionally. Reach out to alumni from your school who work in your target industry. Attend industry events, even if you have to travel. Use LinkedIn to connect with people doing the work you want to do. Ask for informational interviews. The network that elite schools provide automatically must be built manually. It takes time, but it is buildable.

Seek out internships and work experience early. Employers care more about what you have done than where you went to school. A student from a less prestigious school with two strong internships and a portfolio of real projects is more competitive than a student from an elite school with no experience and nothing to show beyond the degree.

Consider whether your target industry actually cares about prestige. Research the backgrounds of people in the roles you want. Where did they go to school? What was their path? The data will tell you how much the school name matters in your specific field. Do not assume. Verify.

The Bottom Line

Where you go to school matters in some industries and matters less in others. Prestige unlocks recruiting pipelines, provides a signaling effect, and offers a built-in network. In technology, creative fields, and skilled trades, demonstrated skills and portfolios matter more than the name on the diploma. From any school, you can build a career by developing a strong portfolio, networking intentionally, and gaining work experience early. The prestige gap is real, but it is not insurmountable. The name on your diploma determines how many doors open automatically. Your work determines how many doors you open yourself.
